   - If problem sizes are very small (e.g., reduction length < 256, GEMM min(M,N,K) < 64, spatial sizes < 8): use PyTorch to avoid kernel launch overhead.
   - For standard conv2d (cuDNN-accelerated), do NOT reimplement; only accelerate depthwise, ConvTranspose2d, or documented special cases.
   - For pure GEMM without beneficial fusion or special transpose handling, call at::matmul in C++ (cuBLAS) and optionally fuse adjacent trivial elementwise ops.

Required file structure and build
- Output a single, self-contained Python file with:
  - All imports (torch, torch.nn as nn, torch.utils.cpp_extension.load_inline, typing, math, etc.).
  - A global cached load_inline build (compile once at import) named "kb_ext":
    - cpp_sources: only forward declarations of the exposed C++ functions (no kernel code).
    - cuda_sources: full kernel implementations and host launchers.
    - functions=[...] listing exactly the C++ function names to expose.
    - with_cuda=True, verbose=False (True is acceptable during debug), extra_cuda_cflags=['-O3'], extra_cflags=['-O3'] (you may add '-lineinfo' for debug, but avoid fast-math that changes numerics).
- Correct headers in CUDA code:
  - #include <torch/extension.h>
  - #include <ATen/cuda/CUDAContext.h>
  - #include <cuda_runtime.h>
  - Do not include <cuda.h>.
  - Use at::cuda::getCurrentCUDAStream() (or c10::cuda::getCurrentCUDAStream()) for kernel launches.

C++/CUDA implementation requirements
- Safety and contiguity:
  - Use TORCH_CHECK for shapes, dtypes, devices, and layout compatibility.
  - Make inputs contiguous in C++ before accessing data_ptr; preserve original dtype and device in outputs (use options()).
- Output allocation:
  - Allocate outputs with correct shapes/strides using input.options() (or weights.options() as appropriate).
- Dtype support:
  - Implement float32 path; for other dtypes, immediately fall back to PyTorch from Python side.
- Performance patterns:
  - Reductions:
    - Block-per-row (or equivalent) mapping for the non-reduced dimensions; grid-stride loop across the reduction axis.
    - Shared-memory partial sums + warp-level reductions (use __shfl_down_sync) to minimize shared memory traffic.
    - Vectorized loads (e.g., float4) when pointers are aligned and remaining length supports it; otherwise scalar tail handling.
    - Typical blockDim.x in [256, 1024]. Use nextPow2 and cap at 1024 for the inner axis length.
  - GEMM-like:
    - Prefer at::matmul for large GEMM to leverage cuBLAS. If you implement a custom GEMM:
      - Use 2D thread blocks and shared-memory tiling (e.g., TILE_M=32, TILE_N=32, TILE_K=32).
      - Loop K in tiles; load A and B tiles into shared memory with bounds checks; __syncthreads; accumulate in registers; unroll inner loops (#pragma unroll).
      - Support transposed operands by indexing into contiguous tensors without performing slow Python-side transposes.
      - Use __restrict__ pointers and coalesced access; write outputs coalesced.
  - Depthwise conv:
    - Map (channel, spatial tile) to blocks; use shared memory to stage input tiles including halo for kernel_size; compute per-thread output pixels; fuse bias/ReLU if present.
  - ConvTranspose2d:
    - Compute H_out/W_out via: H_out = (H_in - 1) * stride_h - 2*padding_h + kernel_h + output_padding_h (similarly for width).
    - Implement im2col + at::matmul (supports stride, padding, output_padding, groups); add bias if provided.
- Stream and launch:
  - Retrieve current CUDA stream: cudaStream_t stream = at::cuda::getCurrentCUDAStream();
  - Choose block/grid sizes to fully cover outputs; avoid underutilization.
- Expose one C++ entry per custom op you replace (e.g., mean_dim, matmul_at_bt, conv_transpose2d_forward, depthwise_conv2d, fused_bmm_addmul).
- Do not define PYBIND11_MODULE manually; rely on load_inline exposure via the functions list.

Mapping Model to accelerated ops (follow cuda_docs precisely)
- Identify the exact op(s) the docs target (e.g., mean over a dimension, MBConv components, ConvTranspose2d, transposed GEMM).
- Replace those ops in forward with the CUDA extension:
  - Reductions: implement mean/sum along the specified axis. Generalize to other axes by remapping grid or pre-permuting (ensure contiguous).
  - MBConv: treat expand/project as GEMMs via at::matmul; implement depthwise as a custom kernel; fuse ReLU6 and residual if applicable.
  - ConvTranspose2d: implement grouped im2col + at::matmul; add bias if provided.
  - BMM + elementwise: implement fast BMM (at::matmul for batched cases if shapes fit) and fuse adjacent add/mul when shapes align.
- For normalization layers (BatchNorm/InstanceNorm/etc.): do not approximate. Either keep PyTorch’s implementation or pre-fuse affine parameters into weights/bias for inference when applicable.

ModelNew integration
- Mirror Model’s __init__ signature; copy or wrap the same layers/parameters/buffers as Model to preserve state.
- In forward:
  - Detect device/dtype; if unsupported by the custom path, route to PyTorch ops.
  - Call the CUDA extension for replaced ops only when heuristics indicate benefit (large enough sizes, supported layouts).
  - Keep all other logic identical to the original Model; ensure overall outputs match numerically.

Robust fallbacks and correctness
- Only float32 custom kernels. For float16/bfloat16/double or CPU tensors, immediately use PyTorch reference ops.
- For extremely small problem sizes where launch overhead dominates, prefer PyTorch.
- Ensure numerical parity by not enabling TF32/fast-math in custom code unless the PyTorch reference uses the same. Maintain default math mode.

Quality and compliance checklist (must pass)
- Single Python file output with:
  - imports
  - global load_inline build named "kb_ext" (cpp_sources declarations only; cuda_sources implementations; functions=[...]; with_cuda=True; -O3 flags)
  - CUDA code includes torch/extension, ATen CUDAContext, cuda_runtime; no cuda.h
  - Use current CUDA stream for launches
  - TORCH_CHECK validations; contiguous tensors before data_ptr access
  - Outputs allocated with correct options() and shapes/strides
  - Shared-memory tiled kernels for GEMM-like ops; warp shuffles for reductions; coalesced access
  - Correct handling for transposed GEMM operands per cuda_docs without Python-side .t() where avoidable
  - ConvTranspose2d via im2col + at::matmul supporting stride/padding/output_padding/groups
  - ModelNew matching Model’s interface and producing numerically identical outputs
- Performance safeguards:
  - Heuristic runtime gating to avoid slow paths (small shapes, unsupported layouts/dtypes)
  - Prefer cuBLAS/cuDNN-backed ATen calls in C++ for large GEMM/ConvTranspose2d when they guarantee equal numerics; fuse only trivial elementwise ops around them if beneficial

Deliverables
- A complete Python file containing:
  - the extension build
  - C++ function declarations in cpp_sources
  - CUDA implementations and host wrappers in cuda_sources
  - ModelNew class implementing the optimized forward with robust fallbacks
- No extra text or commentary outside the code. The file must run with only PyTorch available.

prompts.kernel_gen_prompt:
You are given a PyTorch model class Model. Write a complete Python file that defines a drop-in replacement class ModelNew with the same __init__ signature and forward signature. The goal is to preserve numerical equivalence with the PyTorch reference while accelerating the model by replacing one or more bottleneck operations with custom CUDA kernels compiled via torch.utils.cpp_extension.load_inline.

Strict requirements
- API parity: ModelNew must have the exact same __init__ args and forward signature as Model. It must be a drop-in replacement.
- Numerical parity: Outputs must match the PyTorch reference. Inside custom kernels use float32 for deterministic parity; convert inputs to float32 before calling kernels and cast back to the original dtype at the end if necessary.
- Compile-time correctness:
  - The extension must compile successfully.
  - Do not define a PYBIND11_MODULE manually; rely on load_inline(functions=[...]).
  - Every symbol listed in functions=[...] must be a C++ host wrapper function defined in cpp_sources (and implemented there). Do not list kernel or launcher names in functions.
- Link/runtime correctness:
  - In cpp_sources, call only launcher functions that are actually defined in cuda_sources with exactly matching names and signatures.
  - Use cudaStream_t everywhere for streams; never use CUstream. Do not include cuda.h (driver API).
  - Enforce tensor device (CUDA), dtype (float32 inside), and contiguity checks in C++ wrappers.
  - Allocate outputs with input.options() (or appropriate options) so device/dtype/layout match.
  - Launch kernels on the current PyTorch CUDA stream: cudaStream_t stream = at::cuda::getCurrentCUDAStream();
- Performance:
  - Replace small, bandwidth-bound hotspots. Favor simple reductions and fused pointwise ops over reimplementing heavy operators.
  - Keep heavyweight ops (GEMM/Linear/MatMul/BMM and standard Conv/GroupNorm/BatchNorm) in PyTorch; accelerate adjacent light ops (reductions, InstanceNorm stats, residual add, scale/multiply) with custom kernels.
  - Do not attempt to beat cuBLAS/cuDNN by re-implementing full GEMM/Conv; instead, fuse surrounding elementwise work and reductions to cut memory traffic and kernel launches.

Decide what to accelerate (priority order; choose one or two that clearly appear in Model.forward)
- If the model has InstanceNorm-like behavior (per-instance, per-channel normalization over spatial dims):
  - Implement two kernels:
    1) A reduction kernel that computes mean (and optionally variance) per (N,C) across remaining dims.
    2) An apply kernel that normalizes and applies affine scale/bias, optionally fusing residual add and/or multiply that immediately follow.
- If the model has chains of pointwise ops (e.g., y = a*x + b*residual; or y = (x + bias) * scale):
  - Implement a single fused elementwise kernel that reads each element once, computes the chain, and writes once.
- If the model uses standard Conv/Norm/Activation/Pooling:
  - Keep Conv/GroupNorm/BatchNorm as PyTorch ops.
  - Accelerate lightweight reductions (e.g., per-batch mean across [C,D,H,W]) or fuse adjacent pointwise ops.
- Only if the model explicitly uses ConvTranspose3d (transposed convolution) and it is the core custom op:
  - Implement a correct forward CUDA kernel (naive but correct is acceptable). Compute output sizes via:
    Do = (Di - 1) * stride_d - 2*pad_d + dil_d*(kD - 1) + 1
    Ho = (Hi - 1) * stride_h - 2*pad_h + dil_h*(kH - 1) + 1
    Wo = (Wi - 1) * stride_w - 2*pad_w + dil_w*(kW - 1) + 1
- Avoid reimplementing GEMM/BMM/MatMul/Linear. If the model includes GEMM plus simple scale/add/mul/BN, keep GEMM in PyTorch and implement:
  - A fused post-GEMM elementwise kernel (e.g., scale + residual add + multiply), and/or
  - A reduction kernel to compute BN/IN statistics, followed by an apply kernel.

Mandatory load_inline pattern (must follow exactly)
- Structure:
  - cpp_sources: C++ host wrappers that:
    - #include <torch/extension.h>
    - #include <ATen/cuda/CUDAContext.h>
    - Validate inputs: .is_cuda(), .is_contiguous(), dtype==float (cast in Python beforehand).
    - Compute shapes/sizes and allocate outputs with input.options().
    - Acquire CUDA stream: cudaStream_t stream = at::cuda::getCurrentCUDAStream();
    - Call C-style launcher functions defined in the CUDA source (see below).
    - Return output tensors.
  - cuda_sources: device code and launchers:
    - #include <torch/extension.h>
    - #include <ATen/cuda/CUDAContext.h>
    - #include <cuda_runtime.h>
    - Implement __global__ kernels.
    - Implement C-style launcher functions that take raw pointers/meta-params and cudaStream_t stream, compute grid/block sizes, and launch kernels with kernel<<<blocks, threads, 0, stream>>>(...).
  - Do not include cuda.h anywhere.
- Function binding:
  - In load_inline, functions=[...] must list only the C++ wrapper function names you defined in cpp_sources. Names must match exactly (case-sensitive).
  - Do not include kernel or launcher names in functions.
- Cross-unit signatures (eliminate undefined symbol errors):
  - When a C++ wrapper calls a launcher, declare the launcher in cpp_sources and define it in cuda_sources with an identical prototype. For example:
    // in cpp_sources
    void launch_instancenorm_reduce_kernel(const float* x, float* mean, float* var,
                                           int N, int C, int inner, float eps, cudaStream_t stream);
    // in cuda_sources (definition)
    void launch_instancenorm_reduce_kernel(const float* x, float* mean, float* var,
                                           int N, int C, int inner, float eps, cudaStream_t stream) { ... }
  - Use cudaStream_t (from cuda_runtime.h or ATen/cuda/CUDAContext.h) in both declarations. Never use CUstream. Any mismatch in name or types will cause link/import failures.

Kernel guidance (templates you can adapt)
- Generic 1D elementwise fusion (reads/writes once):
  - Map total elements to a 1D grid, 256 or 512 threads per block.
  - Coalesce reads/writes via contiguous tensors; use __restrict__ pointers.
  - Example launcher declaration:
    void launch_fused_axpby_kernel(const float* x, const float* y, float* out,
                                   float a, float b, int64_t n, cudaStream_t stream);
- Per-(N,C) mean/variance reduction (InstanceNorm statistics):
  - Input shape can be viewed as [N, C, inner] where inner = product of remaining dims.
  - Launch 1 block per (N,C); 256 or 512 threads per block.
  - Each thread accumulates over strided inner elements, then perform shared-memory reduction.
  - Write mean[n*C + c] and var[n*C + c].
  - Example launcher declaration:
    void launch_instancenorm_reduce_kernel(const float* x, float* mean, float* var,
                                           int N, int C, int inner, float eps, cudaStream_t stream);
- Apply normalization + optional fusion:
  - Normalize: y = (x - mean[n,c]) * rsqrt(var[n,c] + eps)
  - Optionally apply affine weight/bias and fuse simple ops: residual add and/or multiply if present immediately after.
  - Example launcher declaration:
    void launch_instancenorm_apply_kernel(const float* x, const float* mean, const float* var,
                                          const float* weight, const float* bias,
                                          const float* residual, float* y,
                                          int N, int C, int inner, float eps,
                                          int fuse_residual, int fuse_mul, float mul_scalar,
                                          cudaStream_t stream);

Python integration (must include all pieces in a single Python file)
- Imports at top:
  - import torch, import torch.nn as nn, import torch.nn.functional as F
  - from torch.utils.cpp_extension import load_inline
  - typing imports if needed
- Define cpp_src (string) and cuda_src (string) that:
  - Contain the exact C++ wrappers and CUDA kernels/launchers you will use.
  - Use only the includes stated above; do not include cuda.h; do not define PYBIND11_MODULE.
  - Match function names and signatures across cpp_sources and cuda_sources exactly.
- Call load_inline once at module import time with:
  - name="custom_kernels_ext" (deterministic)
  - cpp_sources=[cpp_src], cuda_sources=[cuda_src], functions=[...] where ... lists only C++ wrapper names
  - with_cuda=True
  - extra_cflags=['-O3'], extra_cuda_cflags=['-O3', '--use_fast_math']
- Define class ModelNew(Model-like):
  - __init__ signature must be identical to Model.__init__; copy parameters and submodules as needed to preserve numerical parity.
  - forward signature must be identical to Model.forward.
  - Inside forward:
    - Keep GEMM/MatMul/BMM/Conv in PyTorch for correctness and speed.
    - Call your custom kernels for the selected hotspot(s):
      - Ensure tensors are on CUDA and contiguous. Call .contiguous() before passing to kernels.
      - Convert to float32 before calling custom kernels (x = x.to(torch.float32)), and cast the final output back to the original dtype if needed.
      - Pass the current CUDA tensors; do not move between devices inside kernels.
    - For optional fusions, only fuse operations that are algebraically identical to the reference code path.

Validation and safety checklist (must satisfy)
- Do not mismatch function names or signatures between C++ and CUDA sources.
- Do not list non-existent names in functions=[...].
- Do not include cuda.h or use CUstream types.
- Always check is_cuda(), is_contiguous(), and dtype==float in the C++ wrappers; raise TORCH_CHECK failures otherwise (cast in Python before calling).
- Allocate outputs with input.options() or x.options() to preserve device/dtype/layout.
- Launch kernels on at::cuda::getCurrentCUDAStream().
- Avoid device/host synchronization; do not call cudaDeviceSynchronize() in kernels or wrappers.
- No print/logging in the generated code.
- Favor small, focused kernels and fusions; do not attempt to replace highly-optimized PyTorch ops like matmul/conv.

What to output
- A single, complete Python file content that:
  1) Imports everything needed.
  2) Defines cpp_src (string) and cuda_src (string) with the exact host wrappers and kernels you will use.
  3) Calls load_inline with functions=[...] listing only the C++ wrapper names defined in cpp_src.
  4) Defines class ModelNew matching Model’s API, using the compiled extension where beneficial per the guidance above.
  5) Ensures correctness and performance-minded implementation as described.

prompts.kernel_gen_prompt:
Write a complete, single Python file that defines ModelNew, a drop-in replacement for the provided PyTorch Model, that accelerates only the true bottleneck operation(s) using a custom CUDA extension built via torch.utils.cpp_extension.load_inline. Follow these rules strictly to ensure correctness and performance while avoiding regressions:

Primary objectives
- ModelNew must mirror Model exactly:
  - Same __init__ args and same forward signature.
  - Identical outputs (dtype, shape, values within standard float32 tolerance).
  - Preserve parameter and buffer semantics (do not re-register or rename; adding non-registered helper attributes is allowed).
- Replace only the true bottleneck(s). Keep all non-bottleneck parts as standard PyTorch ops to leverage cuDNN/cuBLAS.
- Achieve speedups by:
  - Preferring highly-optimized ATen/cuBLAS-backed paths (at::matmul/at::bmm) inside the extension for GEMM-like work.
  - Using small, coalesced custom CUDA pointwise kernels only to fuse simple elementwise around GEMM (e.g., bias add) or for lightweight transforms (e.g., fused residual add+activation).
  - Avoiding any naive “direct” convolution/GEMM kernels that will be slower than ATen/cuDNN/cuBLAS.

Hard prohibitions (to prevent regressions)
- GEMM-like work (matmul/bmm/linear):
  - Do NOT implement your own tiled GEMM in CUDA. Only use thin C++ wrappers that call at::matmul or at::bmm (cuBLAS).
- ConvTranspose2d/3d:
  - Do NOT write a naive direct kernel that loops/scatters. Only implement a forward via im2col/vol2col + at::matmul and support stride/padding/output_padding/dilation/groups exactly. If you cannot implement this correctly and efficiently, FALL BACK to PyTorch’s conv_transpose op.
- Depthwise Conv2d (groups = in_channels):
  - Do NOT write a naive depthwise kernel. Keep the depthwise stage entirely in PyTorch unless you implement a well-optimized shared-memory-tiled kernel (not required). You may only accelerate the 1x1 pointwise via GEMM (reshape + at::matmul).

Robust fallbacks and fast-path gating
- Only route to the extension when ALL of the following are true:
  - Tensors are CUDA, float32, and .is_contiguous() if passed to any custom pointwise CUDA kernel you write.
  - The op is one of the supported/replaced bottlenecks and the fast path is implemented (e.g., GEMM wrapper using at::matmul/at::bmm, or a simple fused elementwise kernel).
  - Problem size is large enough to amortize overhead.
- Otherwise, unconditionally return the PyTorch baseline result for that op.
- Heuristic thresholds (tune conservatively to avoid regressions):
  - Elementwise/fused add+activation (e.g., residual add + ReLU): use extension only if numel >= 1e5.
  - Pointwise 1x1 GEMM: use extension if N*H*W >= 1e5 and max(C_in, C_out) >= 128.
  - Matmul/BMM wrappers:
    - Only use the extension if you are also fusing an adjacent elementwise op in the same pass (e.g., bias add) OR if max(M, N, K) >= 256 (for 2D) or large batch dimensions for BMM.
    - Otherwise, call PyTorch’s native ops directly to avoid extra boundary overhead.
  - ConvTranspose2d/3d: use extension only if you implement im2col/vol2col+GEMM and output elements per batch >= 1e5; otherwise fallback to PyTorch.
- Unsupported dtypes (non-float32), CPU tensors, non-contiguous tensors for your custom pointwise kernels, or tiny problems: fallback to PyTorch.

Required file contents and structure
- Output a single, self-contained Python file that:
  - Imports everything needed (torch, torch.nn as nn, torch.utils.cpp_extension.load_inline, typing, math, etc.).
  - Builds a single global load_inline extension at import time (cached by PyTorch), with:
    - name="kb_ext" (stable for caching)
    - cpp_sources: only forward declarations for the exposed functions (no definitions, no kernels, no PYBIND11_MODULE).
    - cuda_sources: full implementations (C++ host wrappers + any simple CUDA pointwise kernels; wrappers may call ATen cuBLAS-backed ops).
    - functions=[...] matching the exposed C++ function names exactly.
    - with_cuda=True, verbose=False (True allowed if helpful), and optimization flags (extra_cuda_cflags=['-O3'], extra_cflags=['-O3']).
- CUDA/C++ code requirements:
  - Headers: #include <torch/extension.h>, #include <ATen/cuda/CUDAContext.h>, #include <cuda_runtime.h>. Do NOT include <cuda.h>.
  - Use the current PyTorch CUDA stream: auto stream = at::cuda::getCurrentCUDAStream();
  - TORCH_CHECK shapes/devices/dtypes; enforce float32 for kernels you implement. Support non-contiguous tensors only if you do not access raw pointers/strides manually (ATen matmul/bmm can accept non-contiguous; custom kernels must require contiguous).
  - Do NOT unconditionally make inputs contiguous for ATen matmul/bmm wrappers; pass them through as-is to avoid copies. Only call .contiguous() before taking data_ptrs for custom kernels.
  - Allocate outputs with correct shapes/options (input.options() or weight.options()) and preserve dtype/device. No TF32 or fast-math approximations.
  - No manual PYBIND11_MODULE; rely on functions=[...] in load_inline.
- Expose one function per replaced op, exactly named:
  - bmm_forward(A, B) -> C           // thin wrapper: validates and calls at::bmm(A, B)
  - matmul_forward(A, B) -> C        // thin wrapper: validates and calls at::matmul(A, B)
  - pointwise1x1_forward(input, weight, bias_opt) -> output
    - Implement as reshape [N,Cin,H,W] -> [N*H*W, Cin], weight [Cout, Cin] -> [Cin, Cout], at::matmul to [N*H*W, Cout], reshape back to [N,Cout,H,W], then optional bias add via a small CUDA kernel (contiguous required for the kernel).
  - Optionally (only if fully im2col/vol2col-based and correct):
    - conv_transpose2d_forward(input, weight, bias_opt, stride_h, stride_w, pad_h, pad_w, outpad_h, outpad_w, dil_h, dil_w, groups) -> output
    - conv_transpose3d_forward(input, weight, bias_opt, stride_d, stride_h, stride_w, pad_d, pad_h, pad_w, outpad_d, outpad_h, outpad_w, dil_d, dil_h, dil_w, groups) -> output
  - You may also expose an optional fused residual add+ReLU entrypoint, e.g., fused_add_relu_forward(A, B) -> C, implemented as a simple coalesced elementwise kernel. Use only when tensors are contiguous and large (numel >= 1e5); otherwise fallback to A.add(B).relu() in PyTorch.

ModelNew integration requirements
- Mirror Model’s __init__ exactly (same args; call super().__init__(); create the same Modules/Parameters/Buffers). You may add non-registered helper attributes (e.g., cached transposed weights) for speed, but do not register them as parameters/buffers.
- In forward, replace only the identified bottleneck call-sites with calls into the extension under the gating rules:
  - For Linear/MatMul paths:
    - Default: keep using PyTorch (nn.Linear / torch.matmul / torch.addmm) unless you are fusing bias/activation or the sizes exceed the conservative threshold (max(M,N,K) >= 256). If fusing, prefer the extension to reduce memory bandwidth.
  - For depthwise-separable Conv2d:
    - Keep depthwise conv (groups=C) in PyTorch (cuDNN). If followed by a 1x1 pointwise conv, route only the 1x1 to pointwise1x1_forward when N*H*W is large; otherwise fallback.
  - For residual blocks:
    - Consider fusing the residual add + ReLU into a single kernel call when numel >= 1e5 and tensors are contiguous; otherwise use PyTorch add + relu.
  - For ConvTranspose2d/3d:
    - Only call your extension if you have correctly implemented the im2col/vol2col + GEMM path and the output size is large; otherwise call PyTorch’s conv_transpose op directly.
- Keep the rest of Model’s computation identical; do not change math order beyond safe fusions that are bitwise-equivalent in float32.

Performance-focused implementation notes
- Avoid hidden copies:
  - Do not force .contiguous() before at::matmul/at::bmm wrappers; ATen handles strides.
  - Only require .contiguous() for your custom pointwise CUDA kernels (bias add, fused add+ReLU).
- Reduce overhead in forward:
  - Use simple size/dtype/device checks and threshold gating; no timers or synchronizations.
  - Avoid creating temporary tensors or transposes per-call. If a transpose is needed repeatedly (e.g., Linear weight.t()), you may cache a non-registered attribute like self._w_t that is refreshed lazily when weight.shape/device/dtype changes (do not register it as a buffer/parameter).
- Stream correctness:
  - All kernel launches must use the current CUDA stream; do not call cudaDeviceSynchronize or torch.cuda.synchronize in forward.

Load-inline wiring checklist
- name="kb_ext"
- functions exactly match the C++ function names
- cpp_sources: only function declarations (no definitions, no kernels, no PYBIND11_MODULE)
- cuda_sources: full implementations (wrappers + any simple pointwise kernels)
- with_cuda=True

Follow these rules strictly to ensure both correctness and performance:

Primary objective

- Outputs must be numerically identical to the PyTorch reference (same shapes, dtypes, and float32 tolerance).
- Replace only actual bottlenecks; leave non-bottleneck ops to PyTorch/cuDNN.

- Do NOT reimplement standard conv2d or conv3d (cuDNN-accelerated) with a custom kernel. Only accelerate:
  - depthwise conv (groups == in_channels),
  - ConvTranspose (1D/2D; 3D only if explicitly required and you implement im2col + at::matmul correctly),
  - documented special transpose/BMM/GEMM cases via ATen/cuBLAS,
  - bandwidth-bound elementwise/reduction fusions surrounding those ops (e.g., bias + reduction after pooling).
- If cuda_docs proposes writing a full standard conv3d kernel, prefer the documented “keep cuDNN for conv, add a small fused kernel around it” strategy unless the model’s convolution is depthwise or transposed with clear benefit.

Decision process (strict order)
1) Parse cuda_docs and map Model’s heavy ops to the recommended implementation path with the guardrails:
   - Reductions (mean/sum over an axis or per-batch/channel reductions): implement a high-performance reduction kernel with shared memory + warp shuffles; fuse simple adds/mults/bias when shapes align.
   - Matmul-like ops (GEMM, BMM, including A^T @ B, B^T @ C): prefer at::matmul (cuBLAS) in C++ for large sizes; use a custom tiled GEMM only when you also fuse adjacent bandwidth-bound ops or avoid extra transposes as per cuda_docs.
   - ConvTranspose2d (and ConvTranspose1d where applicable): implement an im2col + at::matmul path that supports stride/padding/output_padding/groups and optional bias; only implement a direct kernel if you can validate correctness; prefer ATen/cuBLAS for the GEMM part.
   - Depthwise conv (1D/2D/3D): implement an efficient per-channel kernel with shared-memory tiling; fuse bias/ReLU if present.
   - Complex fusions: restrict fusion to bandwidth-bound elementwise ops surrounding the core op (adds/mults/activation/reduction). Do not approximate any normalization.
   - Standard conv2d/conv3d: keep PyTorch/cuDNN; if optimization is needed, only add small fused kernels around it (e.g., post-pool bias+sum) as per docs.
2) Heuristic fallbacks to avoid regressions:
   - If dtype is not float32 or tensors are on CPU: use the original PyTorch ops.
   - If problem sizes are small: reduction length < 256, GEMM min(M,N,K) < 64, spatial sizes < 8, batch extremely small (e.g., N < 4): use PyTorch to avoid kernel launch overhead.
   - For pure GEMM without fusion/special transpose handling, call at::matmul in C++ (cuBLAS) and optionally fuse trivial elementwise ops.
   - For post-pooling vectors with shape (N, C, 1, 1, 1): only call a custom bias+reduction kernel if C >= 256 (or a doc-specified threshold); otherwise, use torch.add + torch.sum.
   - For depthwise conv: only run custom kernel when C and spatial sizes are large enough to amortize launch (e.g., C >= 32 and H*W >= 64 for 2D; adjust similar for 3D).

- Output a single, self-contained Python file with:
  - All imports (torch, torch.nn as nn, torch.utils.cpp_extension.load_inline, typing, math, etc.).
  - A global cached load_inline build (compile once at import) named "kb_ext":
    - cpp_sources: only forward declarations of the exposed C++ functions (no kernel code).
    - cuda_sources: full kernel implementations and host launchers.
    - functions=[...] listing exactly the C++ function names to expose; include only the functions you actually call from Python to minimize build overhead.
    - with_cuda=True, verbose=False (True acceptable during debug), extra_cuda_cflags=['-O3'], extra_cflags=['-O3'] (you may add '-lineinfo' for debug, but avoid fast-math that changes numerics).

  - #include <torch/extension.h>
  - #include <ATen/cuda/CUDAContext.h>
  - #include <cuda_runtime.h>
  - Do not include <cuda.h>.
  - Use at::cuda::getCurrentCUDAStream() (or c10::cuda::getCurrentCUDAStream()) for kernel launches.

- Dtype support:
  - Implement float32 path; for other dtypes, immediately fall back to PyTorch from Python side.
- Performance patterns:
  - Reductions:
    - Block-per-row (or per-batch element) mapping for the non-reduced dimensions; grid-stride loop across the reduction axis.
    - Shared-memory partial sums + warp-level reductions (use __shfl_down_sync) to minimize shared memory traffic.
    - Vectorized loads (e.g., float4) when alignment allows; scalar tail handling otherwise.
    - Typical blockDim.x in [256, 1024]. Use nextPow2 and cap at 1024 for the inner axis length.
    - Example exposed function: torch::Tensor fused_bias_sum(torch::Tensor input_2d, torch::Tensor bias_1d) that treats input as [N, C] and returns [N].
  - GEMM-like:
    - Prefer at::matmul for large GEMM/BMM to leverage cuBLAS. If you implement a custom GEMM:
      - Use 2D thread blocks and shared-memory tiling (e.g., TILE_M=32, TILE_N=32, TILE_K=32).
      - Loop K in tiles; load A and B tiles into shared memory with bounds checks; __syncthreads; accumulate in registers; unroll inner loops (#pragma unroll).
      - Support transposed operands by indexing into contiguous tensors without performing slow Python-side transposes.
      - Use __restrict__ pointers and coalesced access; write outputs coalesced.
    - Example exposed function: std::tuple<torch::Tensor, torch::Tensor> vanilla_rnn_forward(...) or matmul_at_bt(...) as per docs.
  - Depthwise conv:
    - Map (channel, spatial tile) to blocks; use shared memory to stage input tiles including halo; compute per-thread output pixels; fuse bias/ReLU if present.
    - Validate groups == in_channels.
    - Expose a function like depthwise_conv2d_forward(...) with stride/pad/dilation support.
  - ConvTranspose2d:
    - Use im2col + at::matmul path supporting stride, padding, output_padding, groups; add bias if provided.

- Stream and launch:
  - Retrieve current CUDA stream: cudaStream_t stream = at::cuda::getCurrentCUDAStream();
  - Choose block/grid sizes to fully cover outputs; avoid underutilization.

- Identify the exact op(s) the docs target (e.g., post-pooling bias + sum, MBConv depthwise, ConvTranspose2d).
- Replace only those ops in forward with the CUDA extension:
  - Reductions: implement mean/sum along the specified axis or channel-sum after pooling. Generalize to other axes by remapping grid or pre-permuting (ensure contiguous).
  - Pipelines like conv3d -> pool -> adaptive_avg_pool -> bias -> sum: keep conv and pools in PyTorch; implement a fused bias+channel-sum kernel (2D [N, C]) with shared-memory reduction; gate it by C threshold.
  - MBConv: treat expand/project as GEMMs via at::matmul; implement depthwise as a custom kernel; fuse ReLU6 and residual if applicable.
  - ConvTranspose2d: implement grouped im2col + at::matmul; add bias if provided; only call custom path when tensors are float32 CUDA and sizes exceed thresholds.
  - For normalization layers (BatchNorm/InstanceNorm/etc.): do not approximate. Either keep PyTorch’s implementation or pre-fuse affine parameters into weights/bias for inference when applicable.

ModelNew integration
- Mirror Model’s __init__ signature; copy or wrap the same layers/parameters/buffers as Model to preserve state.
- In forward:
  - Detect device/dtype; if unsupported by the custom path, route to PyTorch ops.
  - Use runtime heuristics to gate custom calls:
    - Reductions: use custom only if length >= 256 (tuneable).
    - GEMM/BMM: prefer at::matmul; only custom-tiled GEMM if you can fuse adjacent elementwise ops or avoid extra transposes and M,N,K are large (min >= 64).
    - Depthwise conv: enforce groups == in_channels; ensure spatial/channel sizes large enough.
    - ConvTranspose2d: use custom path for larger shapes; else PyTorch fallback.
    - Standard conv2d/conv3d: keep PyTorch/cuDNN path.
  - Call the CUDA extension only for replaced ops; keep all other logic identical to the original Model; ensure overall outputs match numerically.

Robust fallbacks and correctness
- Only float32 custom kernels. For float16/bfloat16/double or CPU tensors, immediately use PyTorch reference ops.
- For small problem sizes or tiny batches, prefer PyTorch to avoid kernel launch overhead.
- Ensure numerical parity by not enabling TF32/fast-math unless the PyTorch reference uses the same (keep defaults). No approximations.

Required extension build and exposure
- Build a single global kb_ext module at import time using load_inline. Expose only the functions you actually use, e.g.:
  - functions=['fused_bias_sum'] for bias+sum reduction,
  - or functions=['vanilla_rnn_forward'] for RNN,
  - or functions=['conv_transpose2d_forward'] for ConvTranspose2d,
  - or functions=['depthwise_conv2d_forward'] for depthwise conv2d.
- cpp_sources contains only the forward declarations for the exposed functions (matching names and signatures).
- cuda_sources implements the kernels and C++ host wrappers with:
  - Includes: torch/extension.h, ATen/cuda/CUDAContext.h, cuda_runtime.h
  - TORCH_CHECK validations
  - Contiguity enforcement before data_ptr access
  - Outputs allocated with input.options() (or weight.options() as appropriate)
  - Launch on at::cuda::getCurrentCUDAStream()
  - Shared-memory + warp-shuffle reductions where applicable
  - Coalesced reads/writes and vectorized loads where safe

Quality and compliance checklist (must pass)
- Single Python file output with:
  - imports
  - global load_inline build named "kb_ext"
  - C++ declarations only in cpp_sources; full implementations in cuda_sources
  - functions=[...] includes exactly the names you implement (no extras)
  - with_cuda=True, -O3 flags, no cuda.h, correct stream usage
  - TORCH_CHECK validations, .contiguous() before data_ptr usage
  - Outputs allocated with correct options() and shapes/strides
  - Shared-memory tiled kernels for reductions and depthwise; warp shuffles for reductions
  - GEMM paths call at::matmul (cuBLAS) for large sizes; support transposed operands by indexing
  - ConvTranspose2d via im2col + at::matmul with stride/padding/output_padding/groups; add bias if provided

- Performance safeguards:
  - Heuristic runtime gating to avoid slow paths (small shapes, unsupported layouts/dtypes)
  - Prefer cuBLAS/cuDNN-backed ATen calls in C++ for large GEMM or built-in convs; fuse only trivial elementwise ops around them when beneficial
  - Never replace standard conv2d/conv3d with a custom kernel; surround with small fusions instead

Implementation templates (you may adapt as needed)
- Fused bias+sum reduction after pooling:
  - Expose torch::Tensor fused_bias_sum(torch::Tensor input, torch::Tensor bias);
  - Treat input as [N, C] via view/reshape in C++; launch one block per N row; reduce C with shared memory + warp shuffles; add bias[c] during load; return [N].
- Vanilla RNN:
  - Expose std::tuple<torch::Tensor, torch::Tensor> vanilla_rnn_forward(...); use at::matmul for the GEMM steps; optionally fuse tanh in a small kernel.

Testing and validation guidance

- Exercise fallbacks (CPU tensors, non-float32, tiny shapes) to ensure correct routing.
- Time both paths and ensure that custom code is only used when it is measurably faster.

  - CUDA implementations and host wrappers in cuda_sources

- GEMM-like ops (matmul, linear, batched matmul):
  - Default to cuBLAS: call at::matmul (or at::addmm/addbmm) in C++ for the heavy multiply unless you must fuse non-trivial elementwise ops that are adjacent and safe to fuse.
  - Only implement a custom tiled shared-memory GEMM when:
    - You need to fuse simple bandwidth-bound elementwise ops into the GEMM for a measurable gain, and
    - You can meet or beat cuBLAS for the given sizes; otherwise call at::matmul.
  - Heuristic: if max(M, N, K) >= 128 (or B*K*N for BMM is large), use at::matmul/at::bmm (cuBLAS). For very small sizes where launch overhead dominates, fall back to PyTorch on the Python side to avoid regressions.
- ConvTranspose2d (including asymmetric kernels and groups):
  - Prefer the im2col + GEMM strategy using at::matmul in C++ (cuBLAS-backed). This is usually faster and simpler to get correct than a direct kernel.
  - Implement full support for stride, padding, output_padding, dilation (if present), and groups. Handle asymmetric kernels (kH != kW).
  - Only write a direct CUDA kernel if you implement it carefully and it is competitive; otherwise stick with im2col + at::matmul.
- Simple fusions around GEMM/convolution:
  - Fuse only simple elementwise ops that are provably exact and adjacent (e.g., bias add, scalar mul/add) if it reduces memory traffic.
  - Do not approximate normalization/stat reductions; keep those in PyTorch unless you can replicate exact numerics.

Leverage problem-specific guidance (cuda_docs)
- You will be given cuda_docs describing the intended computation and best-practice implementation patterns. Follow it precisely:
  - For fused linear/sum reductions like: output = input @ weight.sum(0) + bias.sum(), implement the single-pass GEMV-like kernel they describe.
  - For ConvTranspose2d: follow the im2col + at::matmul guidance, computing H_out, W_out as:
    - H_out = (H_in - 1) * stride_h - 2*padding_h + kernel_h + output_padding_h
    - W_out = (W_in - 1) * stride_w - 2*padding_w + kernel_w + output_padding_w
    - Support groups correctly: split in/out channels per group.
  - For plain GEMM: if you implement a custom kernel, use shared-memory tiling and coalesced access exactly as described.

Required file structure and behaviors

  - All imports needed (torch, torch.nn as nn, torch.utils.cpp_extension.load_inline, typing, math, etc.).

    - #include <torch/extension.h>

  - Use at::cuda::getCurrentCUDAStream() (or c10::cuda::getCurrentCUDAStream()) for kernel launches.

High-performance implementation patterns
- cuBLAS-backed paths (preferred for large GEMM):

  Do = (Di - 1) * stride_d - 2*pad_d + dil_d*(kD - 1) + 1
  Ho = (Hi - 1) * stride_h - 2*pad_h + dil_h*(kH - 1) + 1
  Wo = (Wi - 1) * stride_w - 2*pad_w + dil_w*(kW - 1) + 1
- For other models, pick one or two clear hotspots to replace with custom kernels (e.g., fast elementwise + reduce, simple pooling). Do not attempt to reimplement entire networks.

Mandatory load_inline pattern
- Use load_inline with:
  - cpp_sources: C++ host wrappers that validate inputs, compute shapes, allocate outputs, fetch the CUDA stream, and call launcher functions defined in CUDA.
  - cuda_sources: device kernels and small C-style launcher functions that take raw pointers/meta-params and launch kernels with the provided cudaStream_t.
  - functions: exact list of C++ host wrapper function names you defined in cpp_sources. Names must match exactly. Do not include functions that are not defined.
  - with_cuda=True. Include optimization flags (e.g., extra_cflags=['-O3'], extra_cuda_cflags=['-O3','--use_fast_math']).
- Do not write a manual PYBIND11_MODULE. Do not call torch::wrap_pybind_function yourself; load_inline will auto-bind functions you list.

Compilation/linking checklist
- In C++ (cpp_sources):
  - #include <torch/extension.h>
  - #include <ATen/cuda/CUDAContext.h>
  - Declare and use launcher signatures defined in the CUDA source, e.g.:
    void launch_mean_reduce_kernel(const float* in, float* out, int N, int C, int D, int H, int W, cudaStream_t stream);
  - Define torch::Tensor wrappers with the same names you put in functions=[...]. These wrappers:
    - Check .is_cuda(), .is_contiguous(), dtype==float32 (cast in Python if needed).
    - Compute sizes, allocate output with input.options().
    - Acquire stream: cudaStream_t stream = at::cuda::getCurrentCUDAStream();
    - Call the launcher.
    - Return the output tensor.
- In CUDA (cuda_sources):
  - #include <cuda_runtime.h>
  - Optionally #include <ATen/cuda/CUDAContext.h> (not required if stream is only passed from C++).
  - Implement __global__ kernels.
  - Implement C-style launcher functions (exactly the ones declared in cpp_sources) that compute grid/block sizes and launch kernels with kernel<<<blocks, threads, 0, stream>>>(...).
- Never include cuda.h.

Kernel guidance
- Reductions (e.g., per-batch mean over [C,D,H,W]):
  - 1 block per batch N. Use 256 or 512 threads. Each thread loops over a strided subset of the N-slice and accumulates in registers; perform shared-memory block reduction to one float; write to output[n].
- ConvTranspose3d forward (if required by the model):
  - Naive but correct accumulation by scattering each input element across its output receptive field. Index weight as [Cin, Cout, kD, kH, kW]. Support optional bias. Use 256 threads, grid over N*Cin*Di*Hi*Wi and loop over Cout*kD*kH*kW inside the kernel or vice versa (correctness prioritized).
- Elementwise or small fusions:
  - Favor simple coalesced reads/writes. Use contiguous tensors. Map total elements to a 1D grid with 256/512 threads per block.

Python integration
- Include all imports at top: torch, torch.nn as nn, torch.nn.functional as F, from torch.utils.cpp_extension import load_inline, typing modules you need, and typing hints optional.
- Keep ModelNew.__init__ identical to Model.__init__. Reuse original submodules and parameters where appropriate, or wrap them, to ensure numerical parity.
- Load the extension exactly once at module import time. Use a deterministic module name like "custom_kernels_ext".
- In forward, call custom ops where appropriate; fallback to PyTorch ops for the rest. Ensure inputs to custom ops are .contiguous() and float32 (call .contiguous() and .to(torch.float32) if needed, then cast back to original dtype at the end if necessary).
- Do not add runtime print/debug statements.

What to output
- A single, complete Python file content that:
  1) Imports everything needed.
  2) Defines cpp_src (string) and cuda_src (string) with the exact host wrappers and kernels.
  3) Calls load_inline with functions=[...] matching the host wrapper names.
  4) Defines class ModelNew matching Model’s API, using the compiled extension where beneficial.
  5) Ensures correctness and performance-minded implementation as described.

Common pitfalls to avoid (must follow)
- Do not mismatch function names: every name in functions=[...] must be defined as a C++ wrapper in cpp_sources.
- Do not define PYBIND11_MODULE.
- Do not include cuda.h; include cuda_runtime.h and ATen/cuda/CUDAContext.h as needed.
- Launch kernels on the current PyTorch stream.
- Ensure tensors are CUDA, contiguous, and float32 inside kernels; allocate outputs with input.options().

Now, implement the optimized replacement as described above for the provided Model. Choose a minimal impactful set of kernels to implement based on the model structure (e.g., mean reduction; or ConvTranspose3d forward if applicable). Output only the Python file.

Write a complete, single Python file that defines ModelNew, a drop-in replacement for the provided PyTorch Model, that accelerates only the true bottleneck operation(s) using a custom CUDA extension built via torch.utils.cpp_extension.load_inline. Follow these rules strictly to ensure correctness and performance while avoiding regressions:

Primary objectives
- ModelNew must mirror Model exactly:
  - Same __init__ args and same forward signature.
  - Identical outputs (dtype, shape, values within standard float32 tolerance).
  - Preserve parameter and buffer semantics (do not re-register or rename; adding non-registered helper attributes is allowed).
- Replace only the true bottleneck(s). Keep all non-bottleneck parts as standard PyTorch ops to leverage cuDNN/cuBLAS.
- Achieve speedups by:
  - Preferring highly-optimized ATen/cuBLAS-backed paths (at::matmul/at::bmm) inside the extension for GEMM-like work.
  - Using small, coalesced custom CUDA pointwise kernels only to fuse simple elementwise around GEMM (e.g., bias add) or for lightweight transforms (e.g., fused residual add+activation).
  - Avoiding any naive “direct” convolution/GEMM kernels that will be slower than ATen/cuDNN/cuBLAS.

Hard prohibitions (to prevent regressions)
- GEMM-like work (matmul/bmm/linear):
  - Do NOT implement your own tiled GEMM in CUDA. Only use thin C++ wrappers that call at::matmul or at::bmm (cuBLAS).
- ConvTranspose2d/3d:
  - Do NOT write a naive direct kernel that loops/scatters. Only implement a forward via im2col/vol2col + at::matmul and support stride/padding/output_padding/dilation/groups exactly. If you cannot implement this correctly and efficiently, FALL BACK to PyTorch’s conv_transpose op.
- Depthwise Conv2d (groups = in_channels):
  - Do NOT write a naive depthwise kernel. Keep the depthwise stage entirely in PyTorch unless you implement a well-optimized shared-memory-tiled kernel (not required). You may only accelerate the 1x1 pointwise via GEMM (reshape + at::matmul).

Robust fallbacks and fast-path gating
- Only route to the extension when ALL of the following are true:
  - Tensors are CUDA, float32, and .is_contiguous() if passed to any custom pointwise CUDA kernel you write.
  - The op is one of the supported/replaced bottlenecks and the fast path is implemented (e.g., GEMM wrapper using at::matmul/at::bmm, or a simple fused elementwise kernel).
  - Problem size is large enough to amortize overhead.
- Otherwise, unconditionally return the PyTorch baseline result for that op.
- Heuristic thresholds (tune conservatively to avoid regressions):
  - Elementwise/fused add+activation (e.g., residual add + ReLU): use extension only if numel >= 1e5.
  - Pointwise 1x1 GEMM: use extension if N*H*W >= 1e5 and max(C_in, C_out) >= 128.
  - Matmul/BMM wrappers:
    - Only use the extension if you are also fusing an adjacent elementwise op in the same pass (e.g., bias add) OR if max(M, N, K) >= 256 (for 2D) or large batch dimensions for BMM.
    - Otherwise, call PyTorch’s native ops directly to avoid extra boundary overhead.
  - ConvTranspose2d/3d: use extension only if you implement im2col/vol2col+GEMM and output elements per batch >= 1e5; otherwise fallback to PyTorch.
- Unsupported dtypes (non-float32), CPU tensors, non-contiguous tensors for your custom pointwise kernels, or tiny problems: fallback to PyTorch.

Required file contents and structure
- Output a single, self-contained Python file that:
  - Imports everything needed (torch, torch.nn as nn, torch.utils.cpp_extension.load_inline, typing, math, etc.).
  - Builds a single global load_inline extension at import time (cached by PyTorch), with:
    - name="kb_ext" (stable for caching)
    - cpp_sources: only forward declarations for the exposed functions (no definitions, no kernels, no PYBIND11_MODULE).
    - cuda_sources: full implementations (C++ host wrappers + any simple CUDA pointwise kernels; wrappers may call ATen cuBLAS-backed ops).
    - functions=[...] matching the exposed C++ function names exactly.
    - with_cuda=True, verbose=False (True allowed if helpful), and optimization flags (extra_cuda_cflags=['-O3'], extra_cflags=['-O3']).
- CUDA/C++ code requirements:
  - Headers: #include <torch/extension.h>, #include <ATen/cuda/CUDAContext.h>, #include <cuda_runtime.h>. Do NOT include <cuda.h>.
  - Use the current PyTorch CUDA stream: auto stream = at::cuda::getCurrentCUDAStream();
  - TORCH_CHECK shapes/devices/dtypes; enforce float32 for kernels you implement. Support non-contiguous tensors only if you do not access raw pointers/strides manually (ATen matmul/bmm can accept non-contiguous; custom kernels must require contiguous).
  - Do NOT unconditionally make inputs contiguous for ATen matmul/bmm wrappers; pass them through as-is to avoid copies. Only call .contiguous() before taking data_ptrs for custom kernels.
  - Allocate outputs with correct shapes/options (input.options() or weight.options()) and preserve dtype/device. No TF32 or fast-math approximations.
  - No manual PYBIND11_MODULE; rely on functions=[...] in load_inline.
- Expose one function per replaced op, exactly named:
  - bmm_forward(A, B) -> C           // thin wrapper: validates and calls at::bmm(A, B)
  - matmul_forward(A, B) -> C        // thin wrapper: validates and calls at::matmul(A, B)
  - pointwise1x1_forward(input, weight, bias_opt) -> output
    - Implement as reshape [N,Cin,H,W] -> [N*H*W, Cin], weight [Cout, Cin] -> [Cin, Cout], at::matmul to [N*H*W, Cout], reshape back to [N,Cout,H,W], then optional bias add via a small CUDA kernel (contiguous required for the kernel).
  - Optionally (only if fully im2col/vol2col-based and correct):
    - conv_transpose2d_forward(input, weight, bias_opt, stride_h, stride_w, pad_h, pad_w, outpad_h, outpad_w, dil_h, dil_w, groups) -> output
    - conv_transpose3d_forward(input, weight, bias_opt, stride_d, stride_h, stride_w, pad_d, pad_h, pad_w, outpad_d, outpad_h, outpad_w, dil_d, dil_h, dil_w, groups) -> output
  - You may also expose an optional fused residual add+ReLU entrypoint, e.g., fused_add_relu_forward(A, B) -> C, implemented as a simple coalesced elementwise kernel. Use only when tensors are contiguous and large (numel >= 1e5); otherwise fallback to A.add(B).relu() in PyTorch.

ModelNew integration requirements
- Mirror Model’s __init__ exactly (same args; call super().__init__(); create the same Modules/Parameters/Buffers). You may add non-registered helper attributes (e.g., cached transposed weights) for speed, but do not register them as parameters/buffers.
- In forward, replace only the identified bottleneck call-sites with calls into the extension under the gating rules:
  - For Linear/MatMul paths:
    - Default: keep using PyTorch (nn.Linear / torch.matmul / torch.addmm) unless you are fusing bias/activation or the sizes exceed the conservative threshold (max(M,N,K) >= 256). If fusing, prefer the extension to reduce memory bandwidth.
  - For depthwise-separable Conv2d:
    - Keep depthwise conv (groups=C) in PyTorch (cuDNN). If followed by a 1x1 pointwise conv, route only the 1x1 to pointwise1x1_forward when N*H*W is large; otherwise fallback.
  - For residual blocks:
    - Consider fusing the residual add + ReLU into a single kernel call when numel >= 1e5 and tensors are contiguous; otherwise use PyTorch add + relu.
  - For ConvTranspose2d/3d:
    - Only call your extension if you have correctly implemented the im2col/vol2col + GEMM path and the output size is large; otherwise call PyTorch’s conv_transpose op directly.
- Keep the rest of Model’s computation identical; do not change math order beyond safe fusions that are bitwise-equivalent in float32.

Performance-focused implementation notes
- Avoid hidden copies:
  - Do not force .contiguous() before at::matmul/at::bmm wrappers; ATen handles strides.
  - Only require .contiguous() for your custom pointwise CUDA kernels (bias add, fused add+ReLU).
- Reduce overhead in forward:
  - Use simple size/dtype/device checks and threshold gating; no timers or synchronizations.
  - Avoid creating temporary tensors or transposes per-call. If a transpose is needed repeatedly (e.g., Linear weight.t()), you may cache a non-registered attribute like self._w_t that is refreshed lazily when weight.shape/device/dtype changes (do not register it as a buffer/parameter).
- Stream correctness:
  - All kernel launches must use the current CUDA stream; do not call cudaDeviceSynchronize or torch.cuda.synchronize in forward.

Load-inline wiring checklist
- name="kb_ext"
- functions exactly match the C++ function names
- cpp_sources: only function declarations (no definitions, no kernels, no PYBIND11_MODULE)
- cuda_sources: full implementations (wrappers + any simple pointwise kernels)
- with_cuda=True
- extra_cuda_cflags=['-O3'], extra_cflags=['-O3']
- Includes: <torch/extension.h>, <ATen/cuda/CUDAContext.h>, <cuda_runtime.h>
- Ensure any tensor passed to data_ptr in custom kernels is contiguous; outputs allocated with correct options and shapes.

Quality gates before finalizing
- The file imports, builds the extension once (cached), instantiates ModelNew with the same args as Model, and runs without extra dependencies.
- For Matmul/BMM and 1x1 pointwise:
  - Wrappers call at::matmul/at::bmm (cuBLAS) and preserve shapes/dtypes/devices without forcing unnecessary contiguity.
- For ConvTranspose2d/3d:
  - You either implement im2col/vol2col + at::matmul with exact semantics (stride/padding/output_padding/dilation/groups/bias) and conservative gating, or you always fallback to PyTorch.
- Fallbacks must be used for unsupported dtypes/devices/tiny sizes or non-contiguous inputs to your custom kernels.
- No TF32/fast-math; exact float32 semantics only.
- Avoid regressions:
  - Prioritize leaving PyTorch paths intact for small or borderline sizes.
  - Only use the extension when it demonstrably reduces kernel launches or memory traffic via fusion, or when sizes exceed conservative thresholds.

Deliverables to implement in the Python file
- A single global load_inline extension named "kb_ext" exposing:
  - matmul_forward, bmm_forward
  - pointwise1x1_forward
  - fused_add_relu_forward (optional but recommended for residual blocks)
  - conv_transpose2d_forward, conv_transpose3d_forward (only if implemented via im2col/vol2col+GEMM; otherwise safely unused)
- ModelNew that mirrors Model and routes true bottlenecks to the extension under the gating rules above, with clean fallbacks to PyTorch for all other cases. Ensure outputs are numerically identical to the reference for all supported paths.
